perturbation.original_text: Samples acknowledges that a violation or attempted violation of any of the covenants and agreements in Section 2 above will cause such damage to the Acquiror Companies, the Target Company and the Related Entities as will be irreparable, the exact amount of which would be difficult to ascertain and for which there will be no adequate remedy at law, and accordingly, Samples agrees that each of the Acquiror Companies and the Target Company and any Related Entity shall be entitled as a matter of right to an injunction issued by any court of competent jurisdiction, restraining such violation or attempted violation of such covenants and agreements by Samples, or the employees, partners or agents of Samples, as well as recover from Samples any and all costs and expenses sustained or incurred by the Acquiror Companies and the Target Company and the Related Entities in obtaining such an injunction, including, without limitation, reasonable attorneys' fees. Samples agrees that no bond or other security shall be required in connection with such injunction.
perturbation.changed_text: Samples acknowledges that a violation or attempted violation of any of the covenants and agreements in Section 2 above will cause such damage to the Acquiror Companies, the Target Company and the Related Entities. Samples agrees that each of the Acquiror Companies and the Target Company and any Related Entity shall be entitled to seek remedies.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the guarantee of 'irreparable harm' and 'no adequate remedy at law,' the specific right to an injunction is removed. This is a legal contradiction of the standard enforcement of non-compete agreements, which often rely on the ability to secure injunctive relief to prevent ongoing damage. The omission of 'reasonable attorney's fees' also limits the ability of the company to recuperate the total cost of litigation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Kansas Statutes § 16-119 Covenants not to compete; permitted restrictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: Kansas law permits reasonable restrictions in covenants not to compete. The original clause outlines the remedies available (injunction and attorney's fees) to enforce the agreement. By removing the specific right to an injunction and recovery of attorney's fees, the revised text undermines the enforceability of the non-compete and contradicts the intent of the law, potentially leaving the Acquiror without adequate means to protect its interests. K.S.A. 16-119 permits the use of injunctions, this change might be considered an unreasonable restriction if it eliminates the possibility of injunctive relief.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text provides specific remedies (injunction and attorney's fees) for violations of the non-compete agreement. The changed text removes the guarantee of 'irreparable harm' and 'no adequate remedy at law,' and it omits the specific right to an injunction and recovery of attorney's fees, replacing it with a generalized statement that the company is 'entitled to seek remedies.' The Law Explanation supports that by removing the specific right to an injunction and recovery of attorney's fees, the revised text undermines the enforceability of the non-compete. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is more about the historical context of the law, the Law Explanation directly mentions that K.S.A. 16-119 permits the use of injunctions, implying that eliminating the possibility of injunctive relief could be an unreasonable restriction. This omission directly contradicts the ability to enforce the non-compete effectively, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Samples agrees that, for a period of five (5) years (or such lesser period hereinafter provided for) following the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), he shall not, without the prior express written consent of the Acquiror: (i) except with respect to the Permitted Hotels (as defined below), call upon, solicit, divert, take away or attempt to call upon, solicit, divert or take away any existing or potential customers, suppliers or accounts of the Acquiror Companies, the -2- <PAGE> 3 Target Company or the Related Entities or their respective businesses in connection with any business substantially similar to the Business; (ii) hire or attempt to hire, for himself or on behalf of any other person, any present or future employee of the Acquiror Companies, the Target Company or any Related Entity; or (iii) own, lease, maintain, operate, franchise, license, manage, invest in or provide financing for, or give any advice to any person, firm, partnership, association, venture, corporation or other entity owning an interest in or engaging, directly or indirectly, in the management or operation of, (A) any Hilton Garden, Courtyard by Marriott or Doubletree Club hotel wherever located or (B) any other Upscale Hotel (as defined below) or Extended Stay Hotel (as defined below) located in the United States, Canada, Mexico or the Caribbean within a ten (10)-mile radius of any hotel now or hereafter operated under the "Wyndham," "ClubHouse," or "Homegate" brand, except for any such activity conducted for or on behalf of, or in conjunction with, any of the Acquiror Companies or, in the case of clause (B), Permitted Hotels (as defined below); provided, that if a hotel operated under the "Wyndham," "ClubHouse," or "Homegate" brand is announced for a location that is within a ten (10)-mile radius of any hotel that prior to the date of such announcement was in operation and is owned, managed or operated by any such entity in which Samples has also prior to such date commenced involvement of a type that would otherwise be restricted by clause (B), then this clause (iii) shall not require Samples to cease such involvement; or (iv) enter into any contract or make any commitment to take any action that is restricted by clauses (i), (ii) or (iii) above.
perturbation.changed_text: Samples agrees that, for a period of five (5) years (or such lesser period hereinafter provided for) following the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), he shall not, without the prior express written consent of the Acquiror: (i) call upon, solicit, divert, take away or attempt to call upon, solicit, divert or take away any existing or potential customers, suppliers or accounts of the Acquiror Companies, the -2- <PAGE> 3 Target Company or the Related Entities or their respective businesses in connection with any business substantially similar to the Business; (ii) hire or attempt to hire, for himself or on behalf of any other person, any present or future employee of the Acquiror Companies, the Target Company or any Related Entity; or (iii) own, lease, maintain, operate, franchise, license, manage, invest in or provide financing for, or give any advice to any person, firm, partnership, association, venture, corporation or other entity owning an interest in or engaging, directly or indirectly, in the management or operation of, (A) any Hilton Garden, Courtyard by Marriott or Doubletree Club hotel wherever located or (B) any other Upscale Hotel (as defined below) or Extended Stay Hotel (as defined below) located in the United States, Canada, Mexico or the Caribbean within a ten (10)-mile radius of any hotel now or hereafter operated under the "Wyndham," "ClubHouse," or "Homegate" brand, except for any such activity conducted for or on behalf of, or in conjunction with, any of the Acquiror Companies or, in the case of clause (B), Permitted Hotels (as defined below); provided, that if a hotel operated under the "Wyndham," "ClubHouse," or "Homegate" brand is announced for a location that is within a ten (10)-mile radius of any hotel that prior to the date of such announcement was in operation and is owned, managed or operated by any such entity in which Samples has also prior to such date commenced involvement of a type that would otherwise be restricted by clause (B), then this clause (iii) shall not require Samples to cease such involvement; or (iv) enter into any contract or make any commitment to take any action that is restricted by clauses (i), (ii) or (iii) above.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'except with respect to the Permitted Hotels (as defined below),' significantly broadens the scope of the non-compete agreement. It eliminates an agreed-upon carve-out, making the restriction much more onerous on Samples. If this removal is deemed unreasonable, it violates Kansas law regarding covenants not to compete.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Kansas Statutes § 16-119 Covenants not to compete; permitted restrictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: K.S.A. 16-119 allows for reasonable restrictions in non-compete agreements. By removing the exception for 'Permitted Hotels,' the agreement becomes significantly more restrictive. If a court finds that this expanded restriction is unreasonable in scope or duration, especially given Samples' prior involvement with those hotels, it could deem the agreement unenforceable under Kansas law. The law requires restrictions to be reasonably necessary to protect the employer's business; this alteration removes a previously agreed-upon compromise, potentially rendering the clause overly broad.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes an exception ('except with respect to the Permitted Hotels (as defined below)') from a non-compete clause. The Law Explanation states that K.S.A. 16-119 allows for reasonable restrictions in non-compete agreements and that removing this exception makes the agreement significantly more restrictive. The Perturbation Explanation also mentions this makes the restriction much more onerous, potentially violating Kansas law if deemed unreasonable. The provided snippet (Kansas Statutes § 16-119 (2023)) indicates that this statute is relevant. The removal of a previously agreed upon exception could render the non-compete clause overly broad and therefore an unreasonable restraint of trade.

perturbation.original_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE CONSTRUED, ENFORCED, AND GOVERNED BY THE INTERNAL LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE (WITHOUT REGARD TO ITS CHOICE OF LAW PRINCIPLES).
perturbation.changed_text: THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE CONSTRUED, ENFORCED, AND GOVERNED BY THE INTERNAL LAWS OF A JURISDICTION OF THE ACQUIRER'S CHOOSING.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from a specific state (Delaware) to 'a jurisdiction of the Acquirer's choosing' creates uncertainty and potential unfairness. The Acquiror could choose a jurisdiction with laws unfavorable to Samples, thus potentially violating Kansas law regarding fairness and reasonable restrictions in contracts, especially considering Samples is located in Kansas.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Kansas Statutes § 16-119 Covenants not to compete; permitted restrictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: K.S.A. 16-119 mandates that covenants not to compete must be reasonable. The initial choice of Delaware law, while not Kansas law, provided a predictable legal framework. Allowing the Acquiror to unilaterally select the governing law introduces the potential for the selection of a jurisdiction with laws heavily favoring employers, thereby undermining the fairness and reasonableness required by Kansas law. This could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent Kansas law and impose an overly restrictive agreement.

perturbation.justification: The modification shifts the governing law from a specific state (Delaware) to a jurisdiction chosen by the Acquirer. This introduces the potential for the Acquirer to select a jurisdiction with laws that undermine the fairness and reasonableness requirements of Kansas law regarding covenants not to compete, as explained in the Law Explanation. The modified text enables the selection of a governing law that may favor the Acquirer, potentially circumventing Kansas law (K.S.A. 16-119), as the Law Explanation suggests. The scraped snippet from Kansas Statutes § 16-119 (2023) relates to the meaning of an act which governs contracts and promises. While the snippet itself doesn't directly prohibit the change in governing law, it suggests a regulatory framework exists in Kansas that the modified text could potentially violate depending on the jurisdiction selected.
